summary: My father worked hard to get jobs for his brothers, and Roger was one of them. He got Roger a federal meat inspector position, which was a good opportunity for him. Roger was part of the family network my father supported through his efforts.

Freddie, another of my father's brothers, was helped by him to get work as a meat cutter at Fort Devens. My father was always looking out for his family, making sure they had steady jobs. Freddie's position was part of that broader effort to support his siblings.

Fort Devens was where Freddie worked as a meat cutter thanks to my father's help. It was one of the places connected to the family's employment history. My father also helped others find work in places like the post office, but Fort Devens was notable for Freddie's job.

Lowell, Massachusetts, is where my father became postmaster in 1967, sworn in on St. Patrick's Day by Ted Kennedy. It was a community close to our family, and my father had a strong connection there, managing the post office and later becoming the Middlesex district manager. Lowell was also the place where my father worked for the General Services Administration before becoming postmaster.

My father worked for the General Services Administration in Boston as a liaison officer during JFK's administration. This job required a lot of travel, which my mother didn't like, so he eventually looked for other opportunities. The GSA position was an important step before he became postmaster in Lowell.

John F. Kennedy was president when my father worked for the General Services Administration, and my father was a strong supporter of the Kennedys. JFK's administration was relatively short, but it was during that time my father held the GSA job. Later, Ted Kennedy swore my father in as postmaster, showing the family's ongoing connection to the Kennedys.

Charlie Gallagher was the postmaster of Lowell before my father took over the position. My father applied for the postmastership when there was a turnover, and Charlie was the predecessor. This transition was a key moment in my father's career in government service.

Tewksbury, Massachusetts, was the place where my father and his brothers once went out into the woods during a rainy night to recover stolen business papers after burglars took my grandfather's safe. It was a tough and memorable experience that showed the challenges my father faced managing the family business. That night in Tewksbury was one of many miseries he endured in his administrative roles.

My father had a cooperative education background from Northeastern University, which helped prepare him for the diverse problems he faced running the family meat business and later government jobs. This education contributed to his strong administrative skills and ability to manage complex situations. Northeastern was part of the foundation for his career success.

Daniel Patrick Moynihan is someone I recall quoting when talking about opinions and facts, saying, 'Everyone is entitled to an opinion, but they're not entitled to their facts.' I use that to back up my positive view of my father's life and career, emphasizing the solid facts behind my pride in him. Moynihan's words helped frame how I tell my father's story.

St. Patrick's Day in 1967 was a very special day because that's when my father was sworn in as postmaster of Lowell. Ted Kennedy came to swear him in, and it was a big event with many people attending, even kids being let out of school. The day was snowy, but the ceremony was full of humor and charm, making it unforgettable for our family.

Frank O'Connor was an aide to Ted Kennedy and a good friend of my father. He called my father to let him know that Ted Kennedy would be coming to the swearing-in ceremony, prompting my father to order more invitations. Frank's connection helped make the event even more special and memorable.

The swearing-in ceremony for my father as postmaster took place near the Lowell Memorial Auditorium, in what is now called the Brad Morse Building. It was a significant local venue that hosted this important community event. The location added to the ceremony's prominence in Lowell.

The Brad Morse Building is what the Old Post Office near the Lowell Memorial Auditorium is called now. This was the place where my father was sworn in as postmaster by Ted Kennedy on St. Patrick's Day in 1967. It holds a special place in our family's history because of that event.

Although my father was sworn in during the Kennedy era, his official appointment certificate bore the signature of President Lyndon Baines Johnson in 1967. Johnson was president at the time my father officially took the postmaster position. This shows the transition between administrations during that period.

My father sought a promotion to become the District Manager Postmaster of the Pacific Area, which included Honolulu, Hawaii. This was a significant step up in his career, and it was meaningful because it brought him back to the Pacific islands where he had served in the Marine Corps during the war. Honolulu was part of the area he managed later in life.

Guam was one of the Pacific islands under my father's jurisdiction as District Manager Postmaster of the Pacific Area. It was also a place of personal significance because he had been there during his Marine Corps service in World War II. Later in life, he returned to Guam for the 45th Anniversary of the Liberation of Guam, which was a powerful and emotional experience for him.

My father served in the United States Marine Corps during World War II, seeing combat in the Pacific islands such as Guam and Hawaii. His military service was a defining part of his life, and he often spoke about the beauty of the Pacific. Later, his postmaster career brought him full circle to oversee those same islands, which was a meaningful coincidence.

The Liberation of Guam was a significant event that my father commemorated by attending its 45th Anniversary parade with Peter Tsapatsaris, a fellow Marine's son. The returning Marines were honored in a grand way, and my father described the experience as reminiscent of ancient Roman conquerors being celebrated. It was a deeply moving moment for him shortly before he passed away.

I once read a book called St. Patrick's Cemetery that discussed some tensions between the Irish and the French communities. It was interesting to me because, in contrast, the Irish and Portuguese families in Lowell, including mine, got along very well. This cemetery and the stories around it highlight some of the ethnic dynamics in the area.
